section.title: I Will Remember the Lord's Deeds
section.sectionRationale: A personal lament of Asaph moving from sleepless, unanswered questioning to deliberate remembrance of God's ancient deeds, this psalm models the turn from despair to worship by rehearsing the Exodus itself, God's greatest and most public act of deliverance.
section.thesis: When honest questioning in the night threatens to overwhelm faith, the deliberate choice to remember and meditate on God's mighty deeds, especially His deliverance at the sea, turns doubt into worship even while some questions remain unanswered.
section.frameNote: Four movements: crying out in distress, verses 1 to 4; sleepless, troubling questions, verses 5 to 9; the deliberate turn to remember God's wonders, verses 10 to 15; the mighty deliverance through the sea recounted, verses 16 to 20.

verses.text: For the choirmaster. According to Jeduthun. A Psalm of Asaph. I cried out to God; I cried aloud to God to hear me.
In the day of trouble I sought the Lord; through the night my outstretched hands did not grow weary; my soul refused to be comforted.
I remembered You, O God, and I groaned; I mused and my spirit grew faint. Selah
You have kept my eyes from closing; I am too troubled to speak.
I considered the days of old, the years long in the past.
At night I remembered my song; in my heart I mused, and my spirit pondered:
"Will the Lord spurn us forever and never show His favor again?
Is His loving devotion gone forever? Has His promise failed for all time?
Has God forgotten to be gracious? Has His anger shut off His compassion?" Selah
So I said, "I am grieved that the right hand of the Most High has changed."
I will {{zakar|remember}} the works of the LORD; yes, I will remember Your wonders of old.
I will reflect on all You have done and ponder Your mighty deeds.
Your way, O God, is {{qadosh|holy}}. What god is so great as our God?
You are the God who works wonders; You display Your strength among the peoples.
With power You redeemed Your people, the sons of Jacob and Joseph. Selah
The waters saw You, O God; the waters saw You and swirled; even the depths were shaken.
The clouds poured down water; the skies resounded with thunder; Your arrows flashed back and forth.
Your thunder resounded in the whirlwind; the lightning lit up the world; the earth trembled and quaked.
Your path led through the sea, Your way through the mighty waters, but Your footprints were not to be found.
You led Your people like a flock by the hand of Moses and Aaron.

qadosh.gloss: Holy, set apart, utterly distinct from all else. Asaph's turning point rests on this truth, that God's way is holy, unlike any other, which is why no other god compares to Him and why His deeds can be trusted.

zakar.gloss: To remember, to deliberately call to mind. Repeated three times in verses 10-11, this conscious, chosen act of remembering God's past deeds is the psalm's decisive strategy for moving from despair to worship.

basic.context: In the middle of a sleepless, distressing night, Asaph honestly voices whether God's steadfast love and compassion have run out forever, then deliberately chooses to remember and meditate on God's ancient, mighty deeds, especially the Exodus deliverance through the sea.
basic.hermeneutics: This psalm models an abiding pattern for facing spiritual darkness: honest lament and hard questions are not sin, but the path forward is a deliberate, willed choice to remember and meditate on what God has already done.
basic.originalLanguages: Asaph's despair turns on the confession that God's way is qadosh, holy, utterly unlike any other, verse 13, and his strategy for hope is repeated zakar, remembering, verses 10-11, a conscious act of the will, not a passive feeling.
basic.typology: The path through the sea whose footprints could not be found, verse 19, anticipates Christ walking on the very waters that once obeyed only at God's command, Matthew 14:25, and the whole Exodus deliverance this psalm recounts becomes, in the New Testament, a type of the greater deliverance accomplished in Christ, 1 Corinthians 10:1-4.
basic.christ: Jesus, walking on the stormy sea, Matthew 14:25, exercises the very authority over the waters this psalm attributes to God alone, and the Exodus He recounts, God's people led like a flock through the sea, becomes in Paul's teaching a type of the deliverance found in Christ, who leads His people, the true Israel, through greater waters still, 1 Corinthians 10:1-4.
basic.god: God is shown as holy beyond compare, a worker of wonders whose power over the raging waters and thunderous skies proves He has both the strength and the compassion to deliver, even when His presence seems hidden.
basic.crossReferences: Matthew 14:25 shows Jesus exercising the same authority over the sea this psalm attributes to God. 1 Corinthians 10:1-4 reads Israel's sea crossing as a type fulfilled in Christ. Isaiah 43:16 recalls the same God who 'makes a way in the sea and a path through the surging waters.'
basic.oneStory: This psalm's turn from sleepless doubt to deliberate remembrance of the Exodus threads into the whole story of Scripture's greater deliverance, fulfilled in Christ, who walked on the very waters He once parted and who leads His people through a far greater exodus from sin and death.

context.literary: A lament structured around a decisive pivot at verse 10-11, moving from anguished, unanswered questions, verses 1-9, to deliberate remembrance and a climactic retelling of the Red Sea deliverance, verses 12-20, marked by three Selah pauses inviting reflection.
context.historical: Attributed to Asaph and assigned 'according to Jeduthun,' a named Levitical choir leader, this psalm likely arose in a season of national or personal crisis when God's continued favor seemed genuinely in doubt, resolved by rehearsing Israel's defining deliverance at the Exodus.
context.canonical: This psalm's strategy, honest lament followed by deliberate remembrance of God's mighty deeds, parallels Psalms 42-43 and offers the whole community of faith a pattern for facing seasons when God's presence feels hidden.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To honestly voice the hardest questions faith can ask in the dark, and to demonstrate that the way through despair is a deliberate, chosen act of remembering and meditating on God's proven, historical deeds.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes one specific night of anguished questioning and its resolution; it prescribes, as an abiding pattern, bringing honest doubt to God while deliberately choosing to remember His past faithfulness rather than remaining passive in despair.

debates.question: Does the psalm end in full resolution of Asaph's opening doubts, or does it close with faith standing firm while some questions remain unanswered?

views.label: The psalm reaches full resolution; remembering God's deeds answers the doubts of verses 7-9 completely
views.case: The confident, worshipful tone of verses 13-20, with no return to complaint, suggests Asaph's questions have been fully answered by rehearsing God's power and past faithfulness.

views.label: The psalm ends with faith standing firm even though the specific questions of verses 7-9 are never directly answered
views.case: The psalm never explicitly states 'no, God has not forgotten to be gracious;' rather, it ends abruptly with the Exodus recital, leaving the resolution implicit in remembered faithfulness rather than a direct answer to each question.

debates.bearing: Either way, the psalm teaches that deliberate remembrance of God's proven deeds is the God-given means of sustaining faith, whether or not every specific question receives an explicit answer in the moment.

study.originalLanguages: Asaph's confession that God's way is qadosh, holy, verse 13, unlike any other, undergirds his decision to zakar, remember, verses 10-11, a deliberate, chosen act of the will that turns his despair into worship.

typology.type: A path through the sea, footprints unseen, verse 19
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates Christ walking on the stormy sea, exercising the same divine authority over the waters, Matthew 14:25.

typology.type: Led like a flock by Moses and Aaron, verse 20
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates the greater Exodus accomplished in Christ, who leads His people through deliverance from sin and death, 1 Corinthians 10:1-4.

study.christ: The God whose 'path led through the sea' yet whose 'footprints were not to be found,' verse 19, is revealed more fully in Jesus, who walked upon the stormy sea with the same divine authority this psalm attributes to God alone, Matthew 14:25. Paul explicitly reads Israel's sea crossing, recounted in this psalm's climax, as a type fulfilled in Christ, who leads His people, the true Israel, through a far greater deliverance from sin and death, 1 Corinthians 10:1-4.
study.god: God is holy beyond compare, a worker of wonders whose power over the raging sea and thundering skies is proof that even when His presence feels hidden, His compassion has not, in fact, ceased.

crossReferences.note: Jesus walks on the sea, exercising the same authority over the waters this psalm attributes to God alone.
Reads Israel's sea crossing, recounted in this psalm, as a type fulfilled in Christ's greater deliverance.
Recalls the same God who makes a way in the sea, echoing this psalm's climactic recollection.

study.oneStory: This psalm's turn from sleepless doubt to deliberate remembrance of the Exodus threads into the whole story of Scripture's greater deliverance, fulfilled in Christ, who walked upon the very waters He once parted and who leads His people through a far greater exodus from sin and death.

sermon.title: I Will Meditate on All Your Work and Muse on Your Deeds
sermon.why: Preached from Psalm 77, Piper shows how Asaph's deliberate strategy of remembering, meditating, and musing on God's past deeds turns sleepless doubt and discouragement into worship, calling believers to the same conscious, willed remembrance of God's Word.

quiz.q: What does Asaph do through the night in his distress, verse 2?

options.text: He seeks the Lord with outstretched hands that do not grow weary, though his soul refuses to be comforted.
options.note: This describes his persistent, honest, yet unresolved seeking of God in the night.

options.text: He falls asleep quickly and forgets his troubles.
options.note: The opposite; he cannot sleep and remains troubled, verse 4.

options.text: He gives up praying entirely.
options.note: He continues seeking the Lord persistently, not abandoning prayer.

options.text: He seeks comfort from other gods.
options.note: He seeks the Lord specifically, not other gods, even while struggling to find comfort.

quiz.q: What hard questions does Asaph ask in verses 7-9?

options.text: Whether the Lord will spurn forever, whether His loving devotion and promise have failed, and whether His compassion has ceased.
options.note: These are the psalm's raw, honest questions in the depth of distress.

options.text: Whether he should move to a different city.
options.note: The questions concern God's character and faithfulness, not relocation.

options.text: Whether his enemies will ever be defeated in battle.
options.note: The questions are about God's own compassion and faithfulness, not directly about military victory.

options.text: Whether he has enough resources to survive.
options.note: The questions are theological, concerning God's character, not material resources.

quiz.q: What decisive choice does Asaph make in verses 10-12?

options.text: He deliberately chooses to remember, reflect on, and ponder the works and wonders of the LORD.
options.note: This conscious, willed act of remembrance is the psalm's turning point.

options.text: He decides to stop praying altogether.
options.note: The opposite; his decision leads him into deeper reflection and eventual worship.

options.text: He resolves to seek help from foreign nations.
options.note: No such resolve appears; his turn is toward remembering God's own deeds.

options.text: He decides his troubles are too great to overcome.
options.note: He does not give in to despair; he actively chooses remembrance over resignation.

quiz.q: What historical event does Asaph recount in verses 16-20?

options.text: God's deliverance of His people through the sea by the hand of Moses and Aaron.
options.note: This is the Exodus deliverance, God's defining act of salvation for Israel.

options.text: The building of Solomon's temple.
options.note: The psalm recounts the Exodus sea crossing, not the temple's construction.

options.text: The conquest of Jericho under Joshua.
options.note: The specific imagery of waters, thunder, and a path through the sea points to the Exodus, not the conquest of Jericho.

options.text: The anointing of David as king.
options.note: No such anointing is described; the passage recounts the sea deliverance under Moses and Aaron.

quiz.q: How does this psalm's sea-crossing imagery connect to the New Testament?

options.text: Jesus walks on the stormy sea with the same divine authority this psalm attributes to God, Matthew 14:25.
options.note: This is a direct and striking New Testament echo of the psalm's climactic imagery.

options.text: It predicts the exact size of the future church.
options.note: The psalm makes no such prediction.

options.text: It has no connection to the New Testament.
options.note: Its sea-crossing imagery is directly echoed in Matthew 14 and 1 Corinthians 10.

options.text: It teaches that God no longer performs miracles.
options.note: The psalm and its fulfillment in Christ affirm, rather than deny, God's ongoing miraculous power.

questions: Asaph honestly asks whether God's compassion has ceased forever, verses 7-9. What situation in your life makes you most tempted to ask that same raw question?

His turning point is a deliberate, chosen act of remembering God's past deeds, verses 10-11, not merely a change in feeling. What specific past evidence of God's faithfulness could you deliberately call to mind this week?
Asaph confesses that God's way is holy, utterly unlike anything else, verse 13. How does remembering God's unique holiness reshape the way you view your current struggle?

The psalm recounts God's path through the sea, though His footprints could not be found, verse 19. Where do you sense God may be at work in your life even though you cannot clearly see or explain how?

Jesus later walked on the very waters this psalm describes, exercising the same divine authority. How does knowing Christ holds that same power over your present storm change how you pray about it tonight?

translationNotes.note: This verse is notably difficult in Hebrew and can also be rendered 'To this I will appeal: to the years of the right hand of the Most High,' shifting from a statement of grief to an appeal to God's past faithfulness.

'Your footprints were not to be found' can also be rendered 'were unknown,' both readings emphasizing that God's ways, though powerfully at work, are not always traceable or explainable by human observation.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via biblehub.com/bsb/psalms/77.htm (the makor-scripture toolkit's SQLite database could not be read reliably in this session's sandboxed environment; biblehub's BSB page was used as the documented fallback source). No em or en dashes present. Sermon link verified this session via WebSearch and web_fetch confirming John Piper's 'I Will Meditate on All Your Work and Muse on Your Deeds' at desiringgod.org, preached from Psalm 77.
